message:
beads: sync issue tracker — proptest work assignments

- wa-3nx8: open → in_progress (proptest for event_id.rs)
- wa-yjjb: in_progress → closed (circuit_breaker proptest already
  exists from another agent)
- wa-wiwt: created + in_progress (proptest for survival.rs Weibull
  hazards)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"wa-3kzu","title":"TUI/Web surfaces for saved searches","description":"## What
Expose saved searches in TUI and web dashboards.

## Why
Operators should be able to view/trigger saved searches without memorizing CLI flags.

## How
- TUI: list saved searches, run on demand, toggle enabled
- Web: read-only list + last run status (no mutations yet)

## Risks
- Feature gating: keep behind tui/web feature flags

## Success Criteria
- Saved searches visible in TUI and web
- No direct DB reads from UI; use shared query client","notes":"Implemented saved-search surfaces end-to-end via shared ui_query layer. Added ui_query::SavedSearchView + list_saved_searches(); wired QueryClient/ProductionQueryClient list_saved_searches(); TUI Search view now lists saved searches (enabled/schedule/pane/last_run/error/query) and supports Ctrl+N/Ctrl+P selection, Ctrl+R run (wa search saved run <name>), Ctrl+E toggle enabled (enable/disable when scheduled). Added web read-only endpoint GET /saved-searches returning saved search metadata including last_run_at/last_result_count/last_error with redaction. Added unit tests for SavedSearchView mapping + TUI keybindings. Quality gates passed: cargo fmt --check, cargo check --all-targets, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test; plus feature checks cargo check/clippy -p wa-core --features 'tui web' --lib and targeted tests.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:01:54.854781244Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-11T01:34:52.022733-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T01:34:52.022751-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-3kzu","depends_on_id":"wa-37f9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:22Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-3kzu","depends_on_id":"wa-4enj","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:22Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-3kzu","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.3.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:22Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-3kzu","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.3.7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:22Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-3mfv9","title":"Replace reqwest HTTP client with asupersync::http","description":"# Replace reqwest HTTP client with asupersync::http

## Background
reqwest is the primary HTTP client library, used for outbound API calls. It transitively depends on tokio, so it must be replaced.

## asupersync HTTP client
asupersync provides its own HTTP client built on its reactor:
- No tokio dependency
- Supports HTTPS via rustls integration
- JSON request/response bodies
- Connection pooling built-in

## Affected code
- Any module making outbound HTTP requests
- Feature-gated behind web/MCP features

## Migration pattern
```rust
// Before
let client = reqwest::Client::new();
let resp = client.get(url)
    .header("Authorization", token)
    .json(&body)
    .send().await?
    .json::<Response>().await?;

// After
let client = asupersync::http::Client::new();
let resp = client.get(cx, url)
    .header("Authorization", token)
    .json(&body)
    .send().await?
    .json::<Response>().await?;
```

## Acceptance criteria
- reqwest removed from Cargo.toml
- All HTTP requests use asupersync::http
- TLS works for HTTPS endpoints
- JSON serialization/deserialization works
- Timeout/retry behavior preserved

## Benchmark requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks for HTTP request latency**: Add `benches/http_client.rs` measuring:
  - HTTP GET request latency to localhost (minimal network overhead)
  - HTTPS GET request latency (includes TLS handshake)
  - JSON POST request latency with body serialization (varying body sizes: 1KB, 10KB, 100KB)
  - Connection reuse: first request vs subsequent request latency (connection pooling benefit)
  - Concurrent request throughput: requests/sec with N=1,2,4,8 concurrent requests
  - Comparison with reqwest for equivalent operations (same endpoints, same payloads)

## Property-based testing
- **Proptest for HTTP response parsing**: Use proptest to generate arbitrary HTTP response bodies and verify:
  - JSON deserialization correctly handles all valid JSON structures (nested objects, arrays, unicode, escapes)
  - Malformed JSON produces appropriate error variants (not panics)
  - Content-Length header matches actual body length for generated payloads
  - Response status codes are correctly mapped to Outcome variants (2xx → Ok, 4xx/5xx → Err)
  - Timeout responses correctly produce Cancelled outcome","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:49:11.303308Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:53:17.436769Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-3mfv9","depends_on_id":"wa-1u55z","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:51:58.307925Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"wa-3mus","title":"FTUI-08.3.a Compatibility runbook template per terminal/mux environment","description":"## Background
Compatibility certification must be repeatable across terminal/mux environments with uniform evidence quality.

## Deliverables
- runbook template defining environment metadata, test scope, expected outcomes, and failure policy
- required evidence checklist (logs, snapshots, command outputs, known limitations)
- comparison rubric for pass/conditional-pass/fail decisions

Implement token-based authentication and capability scopes for local IPC.

 {"id":"wa-3pr0","title":"Cross-Pane Correlation Engine","description":"# Cross-Pane Correlation Engine

## Goal
Detect simultaneous or causally-related events across multiple panes using statistical correlation analysis (chi-squared co-occurrence testing).

## How it works
When events occur across multiple panes within a configurable time window:
1. Build a co-occurrence matrix of (pane_i, event_type) pairs
2. Apply chi-squared test for statistical significance
3. Report correlated events with p-values

## Use cases
- Rate limit hits across multiple agents → shared API key exhaustion
- Error cascades: one agent failure triggers others
- Build coordination: multiple agents hitting same compilation
- Pattern: "whenever pane A errors, pane B errors within 30s"

## Integration
- Receives events from pattern detection engine (patterns.rs)
- Publishes correlation events to event bus
- Feeds into wa-283h4.1 (Causal DAG) which adds directionality

## Relationship to other beads
- **wa-283h4.1 (Causal DAG)**: This bead detects CO-OCCURRENCE (events happen together). The causal DAG detects CAUSATION (event A causes event B, with direction and lag). This is the prerequisite.
- **wa-283h4.11 (LSH error clustering)**: This detects SIMILAR errors across panes. Cross-pane correlation detects SIMULTANEOUS events (which may be different events).
- **wa-1l2o (Circuit breaker)**: Uses correlation output to detect cascades.

## Tests
- Synthetic event streams with known correlations → verify detection
- Chi-squared test correctly identifies significant vs spurious correlations
- **Criterion benchmarks**: correlation check for 50 panes × 10 event types (<5ms)
- **proptest**: random event streams never produce false positives above expected rate
- Integration: inject correlated events, verify correlation event published

## Acceptance criteria
- Co-occurrence matrix maintained incrementally
- Chi-squared significance test with configurable p-value threshold
- Correlation events published to event bus
- <5ms per correlation check for 50 panes
- False positive rate below 5% at p=0.01 threshold","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-09T22:43:41.964036Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-11T01:30:37.059551-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T01:30:37.059551-05:00","close_reason":"Cross-pane correlation engine implemented with chi-squared co-occurrence testing, 23 tests passing","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-3pr0","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-09T22:45:23.312191Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-3pr0","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-09T22:45:39.39782Z","created_by":"jemanuel"},{"issue_id":"wa-3pr0","depends_on_id":"wa-1qz1.5","type":"related","created_at":"2026-02-09T23:16:55.078087Z","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}

 {"id":"wa-wb6","title":"[Human command] `wa backup import` (restore with verification and safety checks)","description":"# Task: wa backup import

## Goal
Safely restore data from backup archives with verification and user confirmation.

## Command Design
```bash
# Verify backup without importing
$ wa backup import backup.wa --verify
Backup verified:
  Version: 0.1.0 (compatible)
  Created: 2026-01-18T14:30:15Z
  Segments: 12,345
  Events: 567
  Checksum: OK

# Dry-run to see what would change
$ wa backup import backup.wa --dry-run
Would restore:
  + 12,345 segments (current: 8,000)
  + 567 events (current: 300)
  Config: 3 differences

# Full restore (replaces current)
$ wa backup import backup.wa --replace
⚠️  This will replace ALL current data!
Current data will be backed up to: ~/.local/share/wa/backups/pre_import_2026-01-18.wa
Continue? [y/N]

# Merge mode (keep newer, import older)
$ wa backup import backup.wa --merge
Merging...
  Imported: 4,345 segments
  Skipped: 8,000 (newer local)
  Conflicts: 0
```

## Safety Requirements
1. **Automatic pre-import backup**: Before any destructive import, create backup of current state
2. **Checksum verification**: Reject imports with invalid checksums
3. **Schema compatibility**: Check version and migrate if needed
4. **Explicit confirmation**: Require --yes or interactive confirmation for destructive ops
5. **Watcher coordination**: Require watcher to be stopped, or use --force with warning

## Error Handling
```bash
$ wa backup import corrupted.wa
Error: Backup verification failed
  Expected checksum: abc123
  Actual checksum: def456
  
Backup may be corrupted. If you trust the source:
  wa backup import corrupted.wa --skip-verify --force
```

## Testing
- Unit tests: verification logic, merge algorithm
- Integration: import into empty DB, import with conflicts
- E2E: full restore cycle, error on corrupt backup

## Acceptance Criteria
- Import verifies checksum before any changes
- Pre-import backup created automatically
- Dry-run mode shows exactly what would change
- Merge mode preserves newer local data
- Clear errors for corrupt/incompatible backups

 {"id":"wa-ye8","title":"Enriched error messages: every error includes actionable remediation","description":"# Task: Enriched Error Messages

## Goal
Transform all wa error messages to include **actionable remediation steps**. Errors become guides, not dead ends.

## Why This Matters
Cryptic errors waste user time and create frustration:
- "Error: DB locked" → What do I do?
- "Error: Pane not found" → Which panes exist?
- "Error: Send denied" → Why? How do I fix it?

Enriched errors answer the "now what?" question immediately.

## Error Message Standard
Every error must include:
1. **What happened**: Clear description
2. **Why it happened**: Context/cause (when knowable)
3. **How to fix it**: Specific, copy-pasteable commands (prefer safe commands)
4. **Learn more**: Link to docs or `wa why` command

## Examples

### Before
```
Error: DB locked
```

### After
```
Error: Cannot start watcher - workspace lock is held

Another wa process may already be running.

To fix:
  - See who is running it: wa doctor
  - Stop the watcher safely: wa stop
  - If the lock appears stale, follow wa doctor's instructions (do not guess)

Learn more:
  - wa why (for decision/audit explanations)
  - wa doctor (for environment + workspace paths)
```

### Before
```
Error: Pane 99 not found
```

### After
```
Error: Pane 99 not found

Available panes:
  1: codex @ /home/user/project
  3: claude @ /home/user/other
  7: gemini @ /home/user/third

Did you mean: wa send --pane 9 "hello" (closest match)

To list all panes: wa status
```

### Before
```
Error: Send denied
```

### After
```
Error: Send denied - pane is in AltScreen mode

Pane 3 (claude_code) is currently running a full-screen application.
Sending input now could corrupt the application state.

To fix:
  - Wait for the application to exit
  - Check pane state: wa status --pane 3

To understand:
  - wa why denied --pane 3

To proceed if you truly must override:
  - use the allow-once approval flow (wa will provide a short code)
  - run: wa approve <code>
  - retry the original action
```

## Implementation

### 1. Error Type with Remediation
```rust
#[derive(Debug, thiserror::Error)]
pub enum WaError {
    #[error("Cannot start watcher - workspace lock is held")]
    WorkspaceLockHeld {
        lock_path: PathBuf,
        remediation: Vec<Remediation>,
    },

    #[error("Pane {pane_id} not found")]
    PaneNotFound {
        pane_id: PaneId,
        available_panes: Vec<PaneInfo>,
        closest_match: Option<PaneId>,
        remediation: Vec<Remediation>,
    },
    // ... more variants
}

pub enum Remediation {
    Command { description: &'static str, command: String },
    Url { description: &'static str, url: &'static str },
    Hint { text: String },
}
```

### 2. Error Formatting
```rust
impl WaError {
    pub fn display_rich(&self) -> String {
        let mut output = String::new();
        output.push_str(&format!("Error: {}\n\n", self));

        if let Some(context) = self.context() {
            output.push_str(&format!("{}\n\n", context));
        }

        output.push_str("To fix:\n");
        for remedy in self.remediation() {
            output.push_str(&format!("  - {}\n", remedy));
        }

        output
    }
}
```

### 3. Context-Aware Remediation
Use runtime context to generate specific commands:
- include actual paths, pane IDs, timestamps
- detect platform for OS-specific commands
- show closest matches for typos

### 4. Integration Points
- CLI error handler uses `display_rich()`
- Robot mode errors include remediation in JSON
- MCP errors include remediation hints

## Testing
- Unit tests: Each error type has valid remediation
- Integration tests: Errors include context-specific values
- Snapshot tests: Error messages match expected format
- UX tests: Remediation steps actually work

## Acceptance Criteria
- All error messages include "To fix:" with specific steps
- Context-specific values (paths, IDs) are included
- Remediation commands can be copy-pasted directly
- Error messages are consistent in format
","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T17:46:27.969274665Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-22T04:26:28.706917403Z","closed_at":"2026-01-22T04:26:28.706826712Z","close_reason":"Implementation verified complete: Every error type (WaError, WeztermError, StorageError, PatternError, WorkflowError, ConfigError) has remediation() method. Remediation struct includes summary, commands (with platform hints), alternatives, and learn_more. render_plain() provides human-readable output. All acceptance criteria met: 'To fix' with specific steps, context-specific values, copy-pasteable commands, consistent format. Previously addressed by wa-bnm.2."}
 {"id":"wa-yi0l","title":"Implement MCP tools (full set) by delegating to robot/core APIs","description":"# Task: Implement MCP tools (full set)

## Goal
Implement the full MCP tool set defined in `wa-nu4.3.1.1` by delegating to internal/robot APIs.

## Principle
**One source of truth**: MCP handlers must call the same internal functions used by CLI/robot mode.
No duplicated parsing/validation logic.

## Tool implementation checklist
For each tool:
- Validate inputs (types, required fields)
- Call internal function
- Map result into MCP response schema
- Ensure errors are stable (`WA-MCP-...`)

Tools:
- `wa.state`
- `wa.get_text`
- `wa.send`
- `wa.wait_for`
- `wa.search`
- `wa.events`
- `wa.workflow_run`
- `wa.accounts`
- `wa.accounts_refresh`
- `wa.rules_list`
- `wa.rules_test`
- **`wa.reservations`**
- **`wa.reserve`**
- **`wa.release`**

## Safety
- `wa.send`, `wa.workflow_run`, and `wa.reserve` must enforce PolicyEngine.
- Consider default-deny for any new tool that could mutate state.

## Testing
- Covered by MCP tests (`wa-nu4.3.1.5`):
  - schema stability
  - parity vs robot outputs
  - policy-gated tools denied when appropriate

## Deliverables
- Tool registry wiring for all tools.
- Consistent error codes and `ok/data/error` envelope.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Each MCP tool can be invoked successfully with a trivial request.
- Tools that would act on panes are rejected when policy disallows.


LABELS: area-mcp, phase-4

DEPENDS ON
  → ○ bd-4vx.7: [EPIC] Robot mode CLI (stable JSON envelope + core commands) ● P0
  → ○ bd-nu4.1.1.5: Workflow runner: consume Detection events, select workflow, execute with policy gates ● P0
  → ○ bd-nu4.1.5: [EPIC] Accounts + usage integration (caut as source of truth) ● P0
  → ○ bd-nu4.1.1.9: [Robot] `wa robot workflow` (run workflow by name, stable result schema) ● P1
  → ○ bd-nu4.2.1.4: Pack tooling: `wa robot rules list/test`, pack linter, drift workflow (fixture-first) ● P1
  → ○ bd-nu4.3.1: [EPIC] MCP server via fastmcp_rust (parity with robot mode) ● P1
  → ○ bd-nu4.3.1.2: Implement MCP server skeleton (fastmcp_rust feature, lifecycle, wiring) ● P1
  → ○ bd-nu4.1.6.1: Reservation model + storage schema (pane_reservations) + expiry ● P2

BLOCKS
  ← ○ bd-nu4.3.1.5: MCP tests: schema stability + parity checks vs robot outputs ● P2
  ← ○ bd-nu4.3.1.6: MCP audit integration: record each tool call decision/outcome (redacted) ● P2

 {"id":"wa-ytyo","title":"[EPIC] Phase 3: Full agent support — complete packs + account rotation","description":"# Phase 3 — Full Agent Support

## Objective
Expand from “works for the main path” to “works broadly”:
- Complete patterns and workflows for **Codex, Claude Code, and Gemini**.
- Harden account rotation logic and integrate usage tracking as a first-class system.

## Why this phase exists
Agent CLIs change output formats over time; partial coverage creates fragility. This phase invests in:
- more complete detection packs
- richer structured extraction
- regression fixtures (“golden corpus”)
- workflow variants per agent

## Deliverables
- Rule packs:
  - `core.codex`: full coverage for usage warnings/reached/session summaries/auth prompts.
  - `core.claude_code`: compaction + banner + usage signals (as discovered in field).
  - `core.gemini`: usage reached + session summary + model changes + resume mechanics.

- Workflows:
  - `handle_usage_limits` implemented for all three agents (behavior may differ).
  - `handle_session_end` (capture + store structured session summaries).
  - `handle_auth_required` (centralize login flows).

- Accounts:
  - `caut` becomes the source of truth for usage; wa stores a historical mirror.
  - Selection policy (percent remaining + LRU tie-break).

## Acceptance criteria
- Primary detections for all three agents are covered by unit tests and golden corpus fixtures.
- At least one real-world end-to-end run exists for each agent’s usage-limit handling path (or documented safe fallback if truly impossible to automate).



## Success Criteria
- Rule packs and workflows expand to support all target agents (Codex, Claude Code, Gemini) with consistent behavior.
- Account rotation and agent-specific workflows are covered by regression tests and golden corpora.
- User-facing surfaces (robot/MCP/human CLI) remain stable as capabilities expand.


## Testing
- Golden corpus coverage:
  - Every new rule added in this phase must land with fixtures (positive + near-miss negative).
  - Corpus tests must fail loudly on drift and point to the exact rule + capture that changed.

- Cross-agent workflow regression tests:
  - Use shared workflow engine tests plus per-agent fixtures to validate semantics remain consistent (idempotency, resume, safe failure).

- E2E per-agent reality checks:
  - For each agent (Codex/Claude/Gemini), capture at least one real transcript and ensure it’s represented in fixtures.
  - If true automation is not possible (MFA), require an E2E “fails safe” scenario that still produces artifacts and instructions.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All items in the following sections are satisfied: Success Criteria, Deliverables.
- All Testing requirements pass (unit/integration/E2E) with the logging/artifacts described above.
- Any explicit dependencies for this bead are implemented and validated via tests or E2E artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-yy9z","title":"[Human command] wa stop (stop watcher in workspace safely)","description":"# Task: Human command wa stop

## Goal
Give users a safe, ergonomic way to stop a running `wa watch` for a given workspace.

Many users will run `wa watch` in the background (or via terminal multiplexers). Telling them to manually find and kill a PID is user-hostile.

## Behavior (v0)
- `wa stop`
  - resolves the workspace (flag/env/default)
  - reads the watch lock metadata (pid/started_at/host/version)
  - sends SIGTERM to the watcher pid (preferred) and waits for the lock to be released
  - prints a clear summary:
    - which workspace
    - which pid
    - whether shutdown was graceful

- `wa stop --force`
  - if graceful shutdown times out, escalates to SIGKILL (only with explicit `--force`)

## Safety
- Never kills an arbitrary PID:
  - only act if the lock metadata matches the workspace lock
  - require the process to be owned by the same user (best-effort check)

## UX
- TTY: rich panel output.
- Non-TTY: stable JSON.

## Testing
- Integration test:
  - start a watcher-like process that holds the lock
  - run `wa stop`
  - assert lock is released and process exits

- E2E:
  - `wa-4vx.10.21` validates stop+restart behavior and captures artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `wa stop` reliably stops a watcher started in the same workspace.
- `wa stop` errors are actionable when no watcher is running.
